info.topictitle: A3.3.4 Changing motion in mechanical systems
info.parenttitle: A3.3 Introduction to mechanical systems (HL only)

markdown:
### Mechanical Systems


**Mechanical systems** are designed to **manipulate motion** by adjusting:

1. **Speed**: How fast an object moves.
2. **Direction**: The path an object follows.
3. **Power**: The force applied to move an object.

### Gear-Driven Systems


<definition term="Gear">A **toothed wheel** that **meshes** with another to **transmit motion** or **change speed/direction**.</definition>

1. Use **interlocking toothed wheels** to transfer rotary motion.
2. Can **change speed, torque, or direction** depending on gear size.
3. Common in clocks, bicycles, and car transmissions.

![A small driver gear spinning a larger gear reduces speed but increases torque (ribble cycles)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c0625d3c8ef48d6fb0282e9a8afcf3c35a32a140-3240x2160.png)

<callout type="note">#### Gear Ratios


The **gear ratio** is the ratio of the number of teeth on two interlocking gears. It determines the **relationship between speed and torque**.

$$\text{Gear Ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of Teeth on Driven Gear}}{\text{Number of Teeth on Driver Gear}}$$</callout>

### Belt-Driven Systems


<definition term="Belt-Driven System">A **belt** transfers **power** between at least two **pulleys** — the **driver pulley** moves the belt, and the **driven pulley** is moved by it.</definition>

#### How Belt-Driven Systems Work


1. Use **belts and pulleys** to transfer motion over a distance.
2. Often used where smooth, quiet operation is preferred.
3. Found in **fans, sewing machines, and car engines**.

![Bandsaw has an internal belt system (bandsawblog)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fdcd5f2dd9dd9e024a4ec1b09de670a7658e4e14-550x524.png)

<callout type="note">#### Advantages and Disadvantages


* **Advantages**:
  * Quiet operation
  * Flexibility in layout
* **Disadvantages**:
  * Slippage can occur, reducing efficiency
  * Belts may wear out over time</callout>

### Cam Systems


<definition term="Cam">A **mechanical part** that **converts rotational motion** into **linear motion**.</definition>

1. Use a **rotating cam** to convert rotary motion into **reciprocating** or **linear motion**.
2. Profile shape of cam determines the follower's movement.
3. Common in **engines, automated toys, and machinery**.

<callout type="note">#### How Cam Systems Work


* **Speed Adjustment**:
  * **Shape of the cam**: Determines the speed and distance of the follower's movement.
* **Direction Change**:
  * **Cams** can create complex motion patterns, such as oscillation.
* **Power Transmission**:
  * **Efficiently convert** rotary motion to linear motion.</callout>

![A pear cam converts rotary motion into reciprocating motion, causing the follower to rise and fall in a controlled cycle (BBC)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fc18211388b463dc35321a2c35f0dbb10794ff4e-480x270.png)

### Lever Systems


<definition term="Lever">A rigid bar that **pivots on a fulcrum** to **move a load with an applied effort**.</definition>

1. Already covered in 3.3.3, but important to **reiterate** that levers can change:
  1. The **direction** of force
  2. The **magnitude** of effort
2. Used in tools, lifts, and control systems.

<callout type="note">#### How Lever Systems Work


* **Speed Adjustment**:
  * **Longer levers**: Increase speed but reduce force.
* **Direction Change**:
  * **Levers** can change the direction of applied force.
* **Power Transmission**:
  * **Efficiently amplify** force with minimal input.</callout>

### Linkage Systems


<definition term="Linkage">A **mechanical system** of **connected parts** that **changes the direction** or **size of a force**.</definition>

1. Use **rigid links and pivots** to transmit motion in a set path.
2. Can convert one type of motion to another (e.g. rotary to linear).
3. Often used in **folding mechanisms, robotic arms, bicycle brakes**.

<callout type="note">#### How Linkage Systems Work


* **Speed Adjustment**:
  * **Linkages** can modify the speed and range of motion.
* **Direction Change**:
  * **Complex linkages** can create intricate motion patterns.
* **Power Transmission**:
  * **Efficiently transfer** force across multiple points.</callout>

#### Types of Linkages


<definition term="Reverse Linkage">A **linkage** that **reverses the direction** of a **force or motion**.</definition>

<definition term="Push-Pull Linkage">A **linkage system** that allows **force** to be transferred in **both directions** — when **pushed** or **pulled**.</definition>

<definition term="Parallel Linkage">A **linkage system** where a **central point** is constrained to move in a **nearly straight line**.</definition>

<definition term="Bell Crank Linkage">A **linkage** that **converts vertical motion** into **horizontal motion**, or the other way around.</definition>

![Different types of linkages (firgelli)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e90e1c50817b67ea2e35c0bc8a34519d70d9760b-492x479.png)

****
